text: Why is installing the test automation environment from a version-controlled repository (rather than manually) considered a best practice for verifying the automation environment?

options.text: It allows the team to skip documentation entirely.
It ensures all team members and pipeline agents use the same version of tools and dependencies, providing repeatability.
It prevents the need to test the TAF components themselves.
It allows individual team members to customise their tool versions freely.

explanation: Automated installation from a version-controlled repository ensures that every team member and every CI/CD agent installs exactly the same version of tools, plugins, and dependencies. This eliminates 'works on my machine' problems and ensures repeatability of the test environment setup and teardown. It is a fundamental practice for verifying that the automation environment is correctly configured.

text: Which connectivity check is performed as part of verifying the test automation environment BEFORE running any test suites?

options.text: Running the full regression suite to confirm the SUT is working.
Verifying that the TAF can log in to required servers, access the SUT, and has the correct permissions.
Deploying a new version of the SUT to confirm the deployment pipeline is functioning.
Running exploratory testing sessions to discover new defects.

explanation: Connectivity verification before test execution includes checking that the TAF can log in to required servers, that it has network access to the SUT endpoints, and that the required permissions are in place. These checks confirm the test environment is ready. Running the full regression suite or deploying the SUT are not environment verification activities — they are test execution and deployment activities.

text: A test suite produces different pass/fail results each time it is executed against an unchanged SUT and an unchanged test environment. What is this characteristic called, and how should it be handled?

options.text: Determinism; the tests should be accepted as-is because variation is normal.
Flakyness; the affected tests should be identified and moved out of the main regression suite until the root cause is fixed.
Intrusiveness; the tests should be isolated from each other in separate environments.
Test pollution; all tests should be rewritten from scratch.

explanation: Tests that produce inconsistent results (pass sometimes, fail other times) without any change to the SUT or environment are called flaky tests. Flaky tests undermine trust in the test suite and should be identified and moved out of the main regression suite. The root cause (e.g., timing issues, test ordering dependency, resource contention) should be investigated and resolved before returning them to the suite.

text: What does 'intrusiveness' mean in the context of verifying test suite behaviour, and why is it a problem?

options.text: Tests that take too long to execute, making the pipeline slow.
Tight coupling between automated tests and the SUT's internals, meaning test interactions can cause failures that would not occur during normal production use.
Tests that require elevated security permissions to execute.
Tests that generate too many log entries, filling up disk storage.

explanation: Intrusiveness refers to the degree to which the automated tests interfere with or are tightly coupled to the SUT in ways that do not reflect normal production usage. An intrusive test can trigger failures in the SUT that would never occur during real use, or it can depend on internal implementation details that are not stable. Reducing intrusiveness improves the validity of test results.

text: When investigating unexpected test failures, which of the following approaches is MOST systematic for determining the root cause?

options.text: Delete the failing tests and recreate them from scratch to see if the problem recurs.
Inspect logs and performance data, execute tests in isolation, monitor system resources, and use a debugger to trace the failure.
Assume all failures indicate SUT defects and log them all in the defect tracker.
Wait until the next sprint and see if the failures resolve themselves.

explanation: Systematic root cause analysis for unexpected test failures involves: inspecting test and SUT logs, reviewing performance data, executing the failing test in isolation to eliminate interference from other tests, monitoring system resources (CPU, memory, network), and using a debugger to trace execution. This approach identifies whether the root cause is in the SUT, the TAS, or the infrastructure.

text: A test consistently passes without detecting any defects even though known defects exist in the SUT. Investigation reveals no assertions are present in the test. What should be done?

options.text: Accept the test as passing — if it runs without errors it is valid.
Add the appropriate assertions to the test so that it correctly evaluates expected vs actual outcomes.
Mark the test as inconclusive in the test management tool and move on.
Remove the test from the suite as it is clearly not useful.

explanation: A test without assertions is essentially checking only that the SUT does not crash — it cannot detect functional defects. Tests with missing assertions produce inconclusive or misleading results (false passes). The correct action is to add the appropriate assertions that verify the actual expected outcomes of the SUT behaviour. This is identified during verification of test suite behaviour.

text: Which of the following security risks can static analysis detect in test automation code?

options.text: Runtime performance degradation caused by the SUT processing too many requests.
Plaintext passwords or sensitive credentials embedded in test scripts.
Incorrect business logic implemented by the development team.
Network latency between the TAF and the SUT.

explanation: Static analysis tools can detect security violations in source code, including hardcoded or plaintext passwords and credentials in test scripts. This is a particular concern in DevSecOps pipelines. Static analysis examines code without executing it, so it cannot detect runtime issues such as performance degradation or network latency. Business logic defects in the SUT require test execution to surface.

text: At which point in the SDLC is static analysis MOST valuable for detecting vulnerabilities in the Test Automation Solution?

options.text: Only after the product has been released to production, as a post-release audit.
Early in the SDLC, integrated into CI/CD pipelines, to catch issues as soon as code is committed.
Only during the acceptance testing phase, performed by business stakeholders.
Once per year during an annual security review.

explanation: Static analysis is most valuable when applied early in the SDLC — ideally integrated into CI/CD pipelines so that issues are detected as soon as code is committed. Early detection reduces the cost of fixing defects. Running static analysis only after production release or annually is too late to be cost-effective and misses the shift-left testing benefit.

text: Static analysis applied to the Test Automation Solution categorises detected issues by severity. Which severity level typically indicates an issue that must be fixed immediately and may represent a critical security vulnerability or show-stopper defect?

options.text: Low
Medium
High
Critical

explanation: Static analysis tools categorise findings by severity: Critical issues are the most severe, representing show-stopping defects or significant security vulnerabilities that must be addressed immediately. The severity hierarchy is typically: Critical > High > Medium > Low. Critical findings in test automation code (such as exposed credentials or severe logic errors) should be treated as blocking issues.

text: Your team has developed a TAS for a computer-aided design (CAD) software program. This software has several different versions in production and has been ported to different languages and platforms. Manual testers have been using the TAS on their local computers with different language settings, versions, and platforms. Before proceeding with further automated testing, you need to verify the test automation environment. What is an important consideration when verifying the environment for this TAS?

options.text: Establish a central repository to verify that all testers are using the same version of the capture/playback tool and can access it correctly.
Create and document a procedure to verify the proper installation of the CAD software on each tester's machine.
Set up a central test environment where the CAD software is installed and verify that automation scripts can interact with it from each local computer.
Use configuration management to verify the consistency of test data and scripts across different environments.

explanation: Establishing a central repository ensures that all testers use the same version of the capture/playback tool, which is crucial for consistency in the test automation environment. Documenting installation procedures is important but not specifically about verifying the test automation environment. A central environment does not address the scenario's need for different local environments. Configuration management is important but does not specifically address verifying the test automation environment.

text: You work in a software development team that requires testing in many different test environments. Your manager reports that the team is spending significant time overcoming false-positive results because the TAS is not configured correctly in new environments. There also appear to be version differences across environments — even new environments are sometimes set up with very old TAS components. Which TWO options would help address this situation?

options.text: Create an automated installation script for the tools and configurations that make up the TAS.
Limit the TAS to only be used in the most important test environments.
Utilize a repository to store the TAS that is accessible to all test environments.
Leverage manual testing to verify that the TAS has been configured properly in all test environments.
Due to time constraints, skip the implementation of component tests for the TAS.

explanation: Creating an automated installation script ensures consistent and correct TAS configuration across all environments, eliminating manual configuration errors. Utilizing a centrally accessible repository ensures all environments pull the same version of TAS components. Limiting TAS usage reduces test coverage. Manual verification is not scalable. Skipping component tests reduces TAS quality.

text: You are working on a project to automate a regression test suite. When the regression test suite was executed manually last time, all the tests passed. But when you execute them via the test automation solution (TAS), you find there are some failed tests. What should you do to handle this situation?

options.text: Analyze log files to identify the root cause of the problem.
Eliminate these test cases from the automated test suite so the remaining tests can pass.
Open a defect for the SUT as the failed tests are indicating an SUT problem.
This is normal because automated tests behave differently than manual tests.

explanation: Log file analysis can identify the root cause of the problem when automated tests fail but manual tests pass. Eliminating test cases will not help solve the original issue. The failed tests do not directly indicate a SUT problem — they should be analyzed first to determine whether the issue is in the TAS or the SUT. The statement that automated tests behave differently than manual tests is false as a general principle.

text: You are preparing to execute a test automation suite for a security-critical application which must fulfill the highest security requirements. Which approach should you follow to verify the test automation code?

options.text: Search the test logs for possible credential data.
Eliminate test cases using sensitive test data.
Execute the test suite slowly and methodically to check if there are any security vulnerabilities.
Use a static analysis tool to identify security vulnerabilities.

explanation: Static analysis tools support identification of security vulnerabilities in the test automation code without executing it. Test logs may not contain credential data present in the automation code itself. Eliminating test cases using sensitive data will affect code coverage. Slower test execution does not reveal security vulnerabilities in the code.

text: You have found that the TAS requires different components depending on the deployment environment. This includes such items as URLs being different, different test data and the use of mocks and stubs. What should you do to make the TAS easily installed in the different environments?

options.text: Ensure all the components are under configuration management with clear versioning and environment requirements
Create a different TAS for each unique environment and maintain each of them as separate entities to avoid deployment issues
Provide the developers with links to the different environments so they can deploy the TAS for you in the various environments making whatever changes are required during the deployment effort
Reduce the use of different test environments and instead just use different versions of the TAS to simulate different environments

explanation: All components of the TAS should be under configuration management with clear versioning and environment requirements. Creating a different TAS for each environment would be a maintenance nightmare. Developers should not be deploying the TAS. Using different TAS versions to simulate environments is not a valid approach.

text: You are working on a new TAF, and you are getting some interesting results. When the tests are run under the TAF, they are noticeably slower than when they are run manually. This happens right from the beginning of execution and stays slow throughout. What is a likely cause of this problem?

options.text: There is not enough logging, more should be added for better troubleshooting
The SUT is not adequately provisioned for the proper performance level
The data being used by the test automation is making the tests take too long and should be reduced in size
The TAF is affecting the performance for some reason, more timing information may be needed, but there may be too much logging already occurring

explanation: Since the tests are slower from the very beginning of execution (not just at specific points), the most likely cause is the TAF itself affecting performance. This could be due to excessive logging or other TAF overhead. More timing information is needed to diagnose the exact cause.

text: You have been running a set of 10 tests, but you are getting inconsistent results from the tests. What type of 'correct' behavior is missing?

options.text: Repeatability
Intrusion
Composition
Visibility

explanation: Since you are not getting the same results consistently, these tests are not repeatable. Repeatability means that tests produce the same results when run against the same version of the SUT under the same conditions.

text: What do you check when you are checking for completeness of a test automation suite?

options.text: All tests pass
Approximately 10% of the tests fail consistently
The test data is correct and available
The tests are repeatable across different SUT versions

explanation: Completeness (composition) of a test automation suite requires checking that the test data is correct and available. All tests passing (A) or a fixed failure percentage (B) are not feasible completeness criteria. Repeatability across SUT versions (D) relates to repeatability rather than completeness.

text: Which of the following would be sufficient to determine that an action has actually occurred?

options.text: Add a user and verify that the user can no longer log in
Add a user and then delete that user
Add a user, delete the user, add the user again
Add a user and verify in the database that the user exists

explanation: Verifying directly in the database that the user exists is the only option that actually validates the action occurred. The other options could mask false positives caused by other failures — for example, the delete in option B could fail silently, making it appear the add worked when something else went wrong.

text: Which of the following is a benefit from using a static analysis tool?

options.text: The code will run faster
The code will have extensive logging
The developer will be given suggestions for fixes to problems
The tool will automatically generate defect reports for any found problems

explanation: Static analysis tools give developers suggestions for fixing problems found in the code. Automatic defect report generation can often be turned on, but it is not a recommended practice as it may flood the defect tracking system with issues that won't be fixed, such as code formatting issues.

text: You are about to verify an automated test suite. During the verification process you have found that some test scripts pass one time and fail at another, therefore not providing reliable test results. What should you do to verify the validity of your test scripts?

options.text: This is due to the parallel execution of the test scripts; synchronization would solve the problem.
Re-execute the automated test suite and analyze the test results again.
Remove the test scripts from the automated test suite and analyze them separately.
This happens because several test scripts are using the same test data, so the separation of test data for each test script would resolve the problem.

explanation: When test scripts are intermittently passing and failing (flaky), they should be removed from the active test suite and analyzed separately to identify the root cause. Simply re-executing does not identify the problem. Assuming parallelism or shared test data as causes without investigation is premature — removal and isolated analysis is the correct first step.

text: You have a test suite containing 25 automated tests that verify the login functionality of an application's home page. The test suite is executed at the end of each two-week sprint cycle for regression test purposes. You notice that two test cases out of the 25 may sometimes cause a race condition in the application or receive a random error. What action should you take for these two test cases?

options.text: Take no action because sometimes they execute successfully.
Reduce the amount of test cases in the test suite from 25 to 15 and see if the test suite passes with the smaller amount.
Remove the two test cases from the active test suite and analyze them separately to find the root cause.
Replace the two test cases with ones that pass repeatedly so that the test suite still has 25 automated test cases.

explanation: Flaky tests that intermittently cause race conditions or random errors should be removed from the active suite and analyzed separately to find the root cause. Taking no action allows unreliable tests to undermine trust in the suite. Reducing the suite arbitrarily or replacing tests without fixing the root cause does not address the underlying problem.

text: A TAS performs automated UI-level testing of native mobile apps via a client-server TAF: the client runs on-premise and creates test scripts using TAF libraries to recognise and interact with the app's UI objects, while a cloud-hosted server translates commands into device actions and returns results. The device to run on is specified at runtime. You are verifying whether the test automation environment and all TAS/TAF components work correctly. Which activity would you perform to achieve this?

options.text: Manage the infrastructure hosting the server, including hardware, software updates, and security patches.
Check whether the references to the device on which test scripts will run are correctly hard-coded within those scripts.
Check whether the TAF libraries used to recognise and interact with the app's UI objects function as expected.
Check whether all test scripts executed as part of a given test suite have expected results.

explanation: The goal is to verify the automation environment and TAS/TAF components, not the correctness of specific test suites. In a client-server TAF for mobile automation, the automation library layer that locates and interacts with UI objects is critical; checking it confirms the end-to-end automation mechanism (client → server → device → response) functions. Managing PaaS infrastructure (A) is the provider's job; hard-coding device references (B) contradicts the runtime-specified design; checking expected test results (D) is suite-correctness verification, a later step after confirming the environment works.

text: You have successfully verified that a test automation environment and all TAS components are working as expected. Your goal now is to verify the correct behaviour of a given automated test suite that will be run by the TAS. Which of the following should NOT be part of the verifications aimed at achieving this goal?

options.text: Is the connectivity between the TAS and the necessary internal and external systems available and stable?
Does the level of intrusion of automated test tools influence confidence in the suite's test results?
Do all automated tests within the suite always provide the same results across multiple runs?
Are all automated tests within the suite complete in terms of test data, including expected results?

explanation: There are two verification scopes: the automation environment/TAS components (infrastructure, connectivity, toolchain readiness), and the correctness/trustworthiness of a specific automated suite (completeness, determinism, result validity). The scenario states the environment has already been verified, so connectivity belongs to that prior scope and should not be repeated as part of verifying the suite's behaviour. Intrusion level (B), repeatability (C), and data/result completeness (D) are all suite-focused considerations that remain in scope.

text: A TAS running in a single test environment was successfully manually installed and configured from a central repository, with all components at the correct versions, and it has been verified that all TAS components provide reliable and repeatable performance. The TAS will run several suites of regression tests on various SUTs. Your current goal is to complete all preliminary verifications to ensure the TAS works correctly. Which activity would you perform FIRST?

options.text: Create scripts to automatically install and configure the TAS in the test environment from the central repository.
Check whether TAS connectivity to all required internal systems, external systems, and interfaces is available.
Run a given suite multiple times using the TAS to determine whether all regression test scripts always provide the same result.
Check whether all regression test scripts in a given suite have expected results.

explanation: Verifying the automation environment/infrastructure is distinct from verifying test suites' correctness. Since the TAS is installed correctly and its components perform reliably in isolation, the next preliminary verification is confirming the TAS can actually reach the systems and interfaces it needs end-to-end (SUT endpoints, auth services, databases, integrations). Without confirmed connectivity, later suite executions or repeatability checks could fail for reasons unrelated to test logic. Creating installation scripts (A) aids scalability but isn't needed to confirm the already-installed environment; checking expected results (D) and repeatability (C) both assume reachable dependencies, so connectivity should be verified first.

text: An automated test case that should always pass sometimes passes and sometimes fails intermittently (non-deterministic behaviour) in the same test environment, even though no SUT or test automation code has changed. Which statement about the root cause of this behaviour is TRUE?

options.text: The root cause is a race condition that can be identified by analysing the log files of the test case, the SUT, and the TAF.
Determining the root cause may require, in addition to the TAE, the support of others such as developers and system engineers.
The root cause must be instability in the test environment, since no code has changed.
Determining the root cause is certainly easier than if the automated test always failed (deterministic behaviour).

explanation: Flaky (non-deterministic) behaviour can originate from many sources spanning application code, infrastructure, deployment configuration, tooling, and data — boundaries that often require collaboration beyond the TAE role, such as developers inspecting application internals or system engineers analysing resource/network conditions. Asserting the cause is definitely a race condition (A) is too specific and assertive; no code change does not imply the environment is the only possible cause (C); intermittent failures are typically harder to diagnose than consistent ones because evidence is less reproducible (making D false).
